overview_paragraph: Barcelona is at its best in the shoulders—May–June and September–October—when the sea is swimmable, terraces overflow, and the festival calendar peaks: Sant Jordi fills the streets with books and roses in late April, Primavera Sound and Sant Joan's bonfires ignite June, and La Mercè turns September into a week of human towers, fire runs and free concerts. July and August are hot, beachy and crowded (though Gràcia's festa major in August is worth braving), while winter is mild and blue-skied—calçotada feasts, quiet Gaudí interiors, and the Christmas fira glowing beside the cathedral.
January: The Three Kings sailing into Port Vell for the Cavalcada de Reis, sweets raining over Via Laietana, and tortell de Reis for breakfast.
Mild winter sun on the Barceloneta boardwalk, Gaudí interiors without the queues, and sales along Passeig de Gràcia.
February: Carnaval sequins and satire in the streets, Santa Eulàlia's winter festa filling the Gothic Quarter, and candlelit corners in El Born.
Calçotada season in full swing—charred spring onions, romesco-stained fingers, and porrons passed down long tables.
March: Sant Medir, 'the sweetest festival', pelting Gràcia with a hundred tonnes of candy, and almond blossom brightening Montjuïc.
Blue-sky wanders through the Eixample, first vermut hours in the sun, and the seafront paths waking up for spring.
April: Sant Jordi on the 23rd—the whole city trading books and roses, La Rambla one long bookstall, and love in the Catalan air.
Spring light on trencadís façades, Easter mones stacked in pastry windows, and terrace lunches stretching into the afternoon.
May: La Nit dels Museus throwing open doors till the small hours, rooftop bars firing up, and the first brave swims at Bogatell.
Golden evenings on the Montjuïc miradors, courtyard concerts in the old town, and jacaranda shade over Ciutadella picnics.
June: Primavera Sound bass rolling off the Fòrum, Sónar's day-to-night beats, and beach afternoons that stretch toward ten.
Sant Joan on the 23rd—bonfires, firecrackers, cava and coca on the sand until sunrise—summer lit with a match.
July: Grec Festival nights in a Montjuïc amphitheatre, open-air cinema beneath the castle walls, and barefoot chiringuito dinners.
Late paella lunches in palm shade, sea breezes at dusk on the breakwater, and neighbourhood festes majors warming up.
August: Festa Major de Gràcia turning whole streets into handmade fantasy worlds, correfocs sparking after dark, and rooftop nights.
Sants' own festa right behind it, half-empty Eixample mornings as locals flee to the coast, and long warm swims.
September: La Mercè's giants parading and castellers rising in Plaça de Sant Jaume, fire-devils showering sparks down Via Laietana, and free concerts everywhere.
Senyeres on the balconies for the Diada, golden light over the vineyards beyond the city, and a sea still warm for weeks.
October: Castellers season peaking—human towers nine storeys high in the plaças—and the Sitges Film Festival unrolling its fantastic red carpet.
La Castanyada's roast chestnuts and panellets, warm-sea afternoons that refuse to end, and gallery season back at full tilt.
November: The Barcelona Jazz Festival filling the Palau de la Música, misty mornings in Ciutadella, and vermut hour moving indoors.
Chestnut smoke on street corners, quiet Modernisme interiors all to yourself, and the first Christmas lights blinking on.
December: The Fira de Santa Llúcia crowding the cathedral square with caganers and caga tió logs, and lights strung down Passeig de Gràcia.
Torrons piled high in pastry windows, children beating the tió for presents on Christmas Eve, and midnight grapes to see in the New Year.
